free_text: Neutron capture cross section of La-139
Main reference, data given
Revised values of Karlsruhe
cross sections.
3.7 MV Van de Graaff accelerator
The neutron spectrum was produced via the
Li-7(p,n)Be-7 reaction by bombarding a 30-mum thick
Li-target of 6-mm diameter with protons of 1911-keV,
30-keV above the reaction threshold.
A 39-cm3 high-purity Ge-detector.
A Li-6 glass detector for monitoring of the
neutron flux variations.
The resulting neutron spectrum is a continuous
distribution in neutron energy and has a high energy
cutoff at E(n)=106-keV. The reaction kinematics
restrict the emitted neutrons to a forward cone with
an opening angle of 120-degree.
The samples were 7-mm diameter disks that were cut
from a metallic lanthanum foil of natural composition.
The use of an additional lanthanum oxide sample was
turned down, since the La(2)O(3) and La((OH)3) from
white powders, which makes it difficult to define the
stoichiometry and thus the mass of lanthanum in sample
Therefore, the sample was polished and stored in an
argon atmosphere in order to remove and prevent
surface impurities.
The sample was sandwiched between two gold foils.
Summary of irradiation parameters-
activation   mass irradiation time measuring time
mg           hour         hour
Corrections were made for gamma-ray self-absorption.
The summing correction factor for each gamma
transition was obtained by comparing the intensity of
the line in the monoenergetic simulated spectrum to
that in the complex decay spectrum.
Corrections for the variation of the neutron flux and
corrects for the decay during activation.
Results the s- and r-process components could
be more accurately determined, making lanthanum a
reliable s- and r-process indicator in stellar
spectroscopy.
Total error
Error in sample thickness for lanthanum
Error in counting statistics for lanthanum
Error in self-absorption for lanthanum
Error in the detector efficiency for lanthanum
Error in the gamma-ray intensity per decay
for lanthanum
Error in the divergency of neutron flux

EN-MEAN changed to KT - SM
SD: Major corrections in all Subents.
Subent 003 was split in two. Subents 004-006 added.
ERR-SYS < 0.7 mb
Error in self-absorption for lanthanum
The uncertainty in the monitor reaction-
Error in sample thickness for gold is 0.1%
Error in self-absorption is for gold
Error in the gamma-ray intensity per decay
for gold
Au-198 half-life error
Au-198 intensity error
Error in monitor
*By authors* The final La-139(n,gamma)La-140 cross
section was adopted from the result of the third
activation with the smallest systematic
uncertainty = 33.2+-0.7 mb.
Throughout the analysis, the four strongest lines of
the decay of La-140 were studied. Through their
intensities were found to be perfectly consistent with
each other, only the line at 487-keV was considered for
the final analysis.
Text on p.035801-3 from
Phys.Rev.,C68(2003)035801. Data from LA3 measurement,
E=487.02 gamma line, with minimal systematic error
Renormalized.
SD: SF8=MXW -> SPA in REACTION code.
kT = 30. -> 25.keV. BIB updated. Subent superseded.
Energy dependent JEFF cross sections normalized to
reproduce the measured spectrum averaged cross section
and folded by the Maxwellian spectrum.
Table VI from Phys.Rev.,C68(2003)035801
Renormalized.
SD: Subent was split in two. Part of data
moved to Subent 004. COMMENT deleted. Analysis added.
Subent superseded.
Energy dependent JEFF cross sections normalized to
reproduce the measured spectrum averaged cross section
and folded by the Maxwellian spectrum.
In order to calculate the respective uncertainties,
one would need the error in the differential cross
section, which is not provide in the evaluation. See
REL-REF. There any additional errors are omitted except
for the 25 keV value and for the value at 30 keV,
which is commonly used for comparison of astrophysical
(n,gamma) rates. For the extrapolation from 25 to
30 keV an additional systematic uncertainty of 1.2% was
considered.
